transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, daniela.
Hello.
How are you?
I'm good, yes?
Good.
Where are you calling in from today?
I'm from Costa Rica.
Oh, I'm based in Costa Rica, but I'm actually from Minnesota.
Oh, amazing.
Cool.
Cool.
And you're interviewing for engagement manager, right?
Yeah, correct.
Awesome.
And have you spoken, who did you speak to, who have you spoken to so far?
I talked to Tucker before.
It was for a different position.
I don't remember the name right now.
But it was for a different position.
And then he mentioned that my profile might be closer to this position.
So he showed me the job description and everything.
And after that, he coordinated with me.
Perfect.
Cool.
Yeah, well, I can give you a little bit more background on that, on kind of the position and how it fits within GrowthX.
And then we can talk about you.
This should only take about 30 minutes.
So it should be relatively quick.
Cool.
So I lead customer operations at GrowthX, which means I think a lot about how we deliver our contractual obligations to clients.
We're set up as an agency model, but everything GrowthX does is built on a platform that leverages AI workflows to generate content for clients.
So we have a podcast.
Structure, which should be pretty familiar to anybody who's worked in an agency before.

We have managing editors who are essentially responsible for end-to-end production, leveraging the AI workflows, doing the editing, everything from like content generation, keyword gap identification, to publishing.
The engagement manager will manage three managing editors.
Managing editors manage two to three accounts.
So you'll own eight accounts today.
We'll probably scale that to 12 in the new year, probably in like, well, in probably by Q2 of 2026.
So March-ish.
And the engagement manager really owns the relationship.
So there's no editorial work in the engagement manager role.
It's account management, thinking about kind of like the status of the partnership strategy, and helping like kind of tracking performance, making sure we're going in the right direction.
We also have directors.
Directors work in a pooled model.
And so if you need help with kind of setting a new strategy, doing SEO analysis, thinking about like AI growth, and strategies to lean into there, the director team will help with that.

So the, yeah, so the engagement manager owns like renewals, relationship, and kind of all of the pieces relating to account health, though obviously the managing editor and director will help kind of make sure that you're hitting the mark on quality and like long-term performance against goals, things like that.
Okay.
So basically, it's like a fronting role, which is in charge of leading all of the relationship with the clients, making sure that everything that we deliver fits or meets the expectations, right?
Exactly.
Yeah.
And obviously, growing the account and renewals and all that.
Totally.
Yeah, that is exactly right.
Cool.
Well, let's talk about you.
Why don't you give me a little bit of your background and then I'll ask you some questions.
Yeah.
So, well, basically, I'm currently working at Monks.
I don't know if you know it, but I've been working here for the last eight years, actually.
And I started as a project manager and now I work as an account director.
I've been working with several different accounts throughout the years, from Spotify to Uber and Uber Eats.
it, Um,
Sanofi, and Warner Bros.
Discovery, which was the last one that I am currently working with, Marriott's Hotels.
But this is a pretty recent change.
The last account that I worked with for a long time was Warner.

And we focused mostly on social media content, creating always-end content, or creating campaigns from scratch, or supporting our clients with their campaigns and influencers, creating content, organic content, and that sort of thing.
I've been focusing mostly on social media, but I've had experiences with the team that develops, for example, in-app experiences.
We developed one for Spotify last year, no, like two, two, three years, something like that.
And I-
I've always been leading the teams, making sure that we have the correct wave, and we are following that wave, and making sure that all of the expectations are met, and that sort of thing.
For Warner, which is, like I said, my recent experience for a longer period, I was leading a team of almost 40 people.
We were a very, very big team.
I had people in Mexico, in Colombia, in Argentina, in Chile, and Brazil.
And we were working with, I think it was 11 brands, 11 different brands, creating content for all of those brands, and their different social media channels.
So we had a lot of different clients.
We had the Latam clients, and then the Brazil clients, and then we had, the higher,

leadership in that, but yeah, basically, I was, I had a group of project managers that were in charge of working with the rest of the team on a day-to-day basis, but I was very, very involved in everything that happened with the account.
And we actually, we started with a team of 20 people, and what was it, 23, and then in 2024, we managed to renew the contract for 18 months again, and it was for 40 people.
So we grew the account a lot, we went from nine brands to 11 or 12 brands actually, and, and it was a very, very cool experience.
I was very involved in the, you know, understanding, like, what the needs were for that second year of contract, and worked very.
Closely with apps to understand like, okay, where are the gaps right now?
And what are the clients needs and making sure that the new team or the new structure clearly, like, fulfilled those gaps.
But, you know, trying to fit in within the budget and all that.
Yeah, yeah, cool.
That's super helpful.
So just for a little bit of context, growthx is like, strictly organic SEO content.
So like, we're delivering blog content, we're not doing much.
we're not doing any social or video or anything like that, aside from the content we're developing for ourselves.
So do you have, like, do you have experience working on that SEO side?
No, not really.
I work in fantasy mostly on social media, but I'm very open to learning a lot of news.
That's one of the things that I've been looking for.
I'm trying to see if I find new opportunities outside of MUNX, because I've been working for the last three years within the same industry.
So I was trying to see, you know, to find new opportunities to keep learning and keep developing new skills.
Cool.
Yeah.
So can you talk to me about an account that you've managed that has required, like, cross-functional support across multiple players within your company?
Yeah, well, that project for Spotify, we developed, like, a full campaign.
developed, like, we received a brief that the client needed to create an experience for the users.
And we came up with the whole, like, completely from scratch, from the strategy behind the campaign.

The creative approach, and that led to creating this in-app experience, which pulled data from the app, and pulling all the songs and everything, and assigned several different profiles to the users, and that sort of thing, based on what they listened, and that was a 360 campaign.

We worked on the experience itself, we created the whole organic content, we created the ads for it, we had influencers involved, we created several different events across different cities, in different countries, and it was actually, it was a campaign that started, it was a project that was focused on Latam, and then it escalated to U.S.
was really important then?
Spain, and I think some countries in Asia actually participated as well.
So it was a very, very big campaign.
It had a lot of great results, and we were very, very proud.
We, in terms of, like, different players and all that, we had people from strategy, from creatives, obviously data, and the UX.
And, of people that worked on the development of the whole experience.
What else?
Yeah, basically that.
Awesome.
Yeah, that's pretty comprehensive.
Cool.
Can you talk to me about how you structure your work week when you're working on, like, multiple deliverables for multiple clients?
So, basically, I like to have, like, different touch points.

With the clients to make sure like at least once a week to understand like everything that we have for that week or the upcoming weeks and keep a very like specific status that if I have project managers that we review every day to make sure that we are meeting all of the required tasks and to not full like make our calendars even worse than they are.

I try to always keep like reminders on Slack or in our group chats to make sure that everyone is aware of everything that they need to do either for the day or for the week and keep close tabs on what's happening, what everyone is doing to make sure that we are meeting the client's deadlines and all that.
And I also use a lot of like timetables and I've used Monday.
The two organs are different tasks and all that.
Yeah, awesome.
Okay, and then can you talk to me about a time when a client deliverable was going off track and what you did to, like, get it back on the right direction?
Let's see.

Okay, well, particularly with that campaign, that project with Spotify, since it had, like, a lot of different things happening at once, it tend to go over, like, maybe some deadlines we're not getting, or we were not getting, sorry, we're not, like, getting to those deadlines with the deliverables that we needed to do, but that tend to happen because we were missing.
things.
Yeah, I mean,
Some approvals in the process, or the client was not getting us the information that they needed.
So when that, we normally gave like several flags, like, okay, guys, we need to have this information, but by set date, if not, then we're not going to be able to deliver.
And, you know, kept announcing that to make sure that we're covering our bags on that front.
But it some, at some point, it got to the point that we were not able to deliver and set date.
So, but we had like the backup that everything was, you know, written, and we had proofs that we were falling off constantly, and that we were not getting the information.
So, after that, at the end, we needed to, okay, like, take a step back and understand, okay, what do we need to do with what we have if the client gave us the information?
Because we couldn't move forward without it.

So once the client provided that information, we got together and started pulling, like, okay, organizing what needs to be done, like, understanding the process, and, like, having very, very close conversations with the client to make sure that we had the approvals we needed to keep moving forward.
And we actually, like, increased the status with them to two status a week.
had once a week.
We increased it to two to make sure that we were, you know, we had the spaces that we needed, the approvals, and all that.
And at the end of the day, everything got out in time.
So.
That's great.
Yeah.
Cool.
I'm Just making a note.
All right.
I know that was quick.
I only had three questions.
What questions do you have for me?

No, I was wondering, like, well, you told me a little bit about the role itself, but I wanted to see if you could, like, maybe give me a little bit of context of how, like, what's a date and an engagement manager?
Like, what does it look like?
Yeah.
So since you're managing, today we've got, like, weekly calls with clients.
I think we're moving to biweekly cadence.
We're aiming to, but a lot of it will be sort of like Monday communications.
Okay.
Hey, this is what we want to get done this week.
This is what performance looks like.
This is how we're thinking about, like, your strategy.
Have a call with the client.
Really thinking, I think the things that you want to hone in on are...
Like performance of the, of everything that they've, that we've published and talk about kind of like how we're doing against goals and how we can impact those numbers.
And then by the end of the week, talk about, okay, this is what we accomplished this week.
This is what we published.
So it's a lot of coordination of performance, making sure everything's on track.
If anything, if any of the numbers like look like they are becoming alarming, we're missing something, then you'll pull in a director by filing a ticket in our project management system.
Directors will pick up that ticket and then address from there.
So it's a lot of it is like coordination, but it's coordination with strategy in mind.
So making sure that you're doing the most impactful thing for a client at any point in time.
Okay.
Okay.
Got it.
I think that was...
was...
That's basically what I wanted to know.
You explained everything in the beginning, so I think I'm okay.
Thank you.
Awesome.
Well, Danielle, it was great chatting with you.
And yeah, have a good day.
Thank you.
You too.
Bye.
Bye-bye.
